comments: This paper addresses an important and well-motivated shortcoming of neural poem generation—the tendency to produce fluent but unsurprising text—by augmenting an attention-based sequence model with a simple instance memory. The proposed memory is straightforward: store decoder states paired with character embeddings from exemplar poems and bias generation via a similarity-weighted sum. Strengths: the idea is intuitive and interpretable, the paper situates the work within prior memory/NTM approaches, and the authors perform human expert evaluations (with many expert annotators) showing improvements in aesthetic innovation and controllable style transfer. Weaknesses and issues to address: (1) some technical details are underspecified or insufficiently analyzed — selection and indexing of memory elements, memory size K, computational cost at inference, and sensitivity to the choice of β are briefly discussed but not systematically ablated; (2) reporting of evaluation results lacks statistical significance tests and more granular analysis (e.g., per-topic behavior, failure cases); (3) comparisons are limited primarily to a reproduced attention baseline — stronger comparisons or controlled ablations (e.g., learned vs fixed β, random memory, varying memory size) would strengthen claims about what aspects of the memory drive gains; (4) MEM datasets are relatively small (500/300 examples) — it would help to know how performance scales with larger/more diverse memories; (5) possible risks of copying verses from the memory (plagiarism or overly derivative output) are not discussed. Suggestions: include ablation studies on β and memory size and selection; add statistical tests for human evaluations; provide more sampled outputs and analyses of when memory helps vs hurts; discuss computational overhead and any measures to avoid verbatim copying. Overall, the work is a useful, practical contribution to controllable/creative text generation; with the above additions it would be stronger and more reproducible.
